skill.name: xget
skill.description: Helps configure Xget URL rewriting for downloads, registries, package managers, containers, CI/CD, and AI SDK base URLs.
skill.summary: Converts upstream service URLs and integration guidance into Xget-ready configuration.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many execution, network, filesystem, and environment-access patterns. Review confirms most code-execution and weak-crypto hits are false positives from documentation examples, template strings, and parser logic, but the skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to run shell setup, edit project files, and fetch remote README or platform data. No evidence found of credential exfiltration, prompt injection, obfuscated payloads, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed Command Execution and File Modification
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill tells the agent to treat setup and migration requests as execution intent, run shell commands, edit real files, and verify results. This behavior is appropriate for a DevOps helper, but it increases risk if users provide unsafe commands or if the agent applies changes without enough confirmation. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The relevant instructions are explicit and repeated in the skill workflow and reference guide, but they are not malicious by themselves.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution guidance is explicit and semantically confirmed. The risk depends on user intent and agent safeguards rather than hidden malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Remote Fetches From Default and User-Controlled URLs
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The helper fetches a remote platform catalog and README content, and command-line flags can override the source URL and README URL. This is needed for live Xget data, but it can expose the agent to untrusted remote content if a user supplies a hostile URL. Confidence: 0.80. Confidence reasoning: The network behavior is direct and clear, while no sensitive values are sent in the request body.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly performs HTTPS GET requests and allows URL overrides. I did not find evidence that credentials are transmitted.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Shell Profile and Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The reference guide includes persistent shell profile edits for XGET_BASE_URL and lists package manager, container, workflow, environment, and SDK files as modification targets. These changes are normal for configuration work, but they can affect future shells or project behavior. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The file-modification targets are explicit, although the skill does not contain code that writes them automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The persistent configuration instructions are concrete and user-impacting. They remain legitimate when requested by the user.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Limited to XGET_BASE_URL
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for credential access. The script reads process.env.XGET_BASE_URL only to resolve the Xget base URL for conversion and snippet rewriting. No evidence found that it reads secrets, enumerates the environment, or sends environment values to a remote endpoint. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The environment variable name and data flow are visible at both use sites.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both process.env references target the same non-secret base URL variable. I found no broader environment access.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution and Weak-Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported backtick and weak-cryptography patterns map to JavaScript template literals, Markdown code fences, string matching, parser helpers, and documentation examples. No evidence found of eval, Function constructors, child_process usage, shell backtick execution, or weak cryptographic operations. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: Targeted review of the flagged script paths found parsing and formatting logic instead of executable shell or crypto APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are not dynamic execution or cryptographic code. Some surrounding documentation still describes commands users may run.

dangerous_patterns.title: Execution-First Skill Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the agent to execute setup, migration, deployment, and configuration tasks instead of only returning examples. This is a powerful workflow pattern that should be paired with normal user-consent checks before modifying persistent files. Confidence: 0.87. Confidence reasoning: The execution-first instruction is explicit in the skill body.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is direct and repeated later in the workflow. It is risky but not inherently malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Remote Source Options
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI accepts source and README URL overrides that are fetched over the network. A malicious remote README could influence generated guidance if the user directs the agent to use it. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: Override flags are clear, but exploitation requires user-supplied untrusted URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch sources can be overridden. The default URLs are ordinary project resources.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Shell Startup File Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference guide includes commands that append XGET_BASE_URL to shell startup files. This is expected setup behavior, but persistent profile changes can surprise users if applied without a clear request. Confidence: 0.83. Confidence reasoning: The commands and target files are plainly documented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The profile-write examples are explicit. They are configuration changes rather than stealth persistence.

content.value_statement: Developers often need faster access to upstream package, container, and API resources. This skill helps convert URLs and apply Xget configuration across common tools.
actual_capabilities: Resolves an Xget base URL from a command option or XGET_BASE_URL.
Fetches the live Xget platform catalog and lists supported prefixes.
Converts supported upstream URLs into Xget-prefixed URLs.
Fetches README use-case headings and relevant setup snippets.
Rewrites public Xget examples to a user-provided base URL.
Guides updates for package managers, containers, CI/CD, and AI SDK settings.
limitations: Requires network access to refresh live platform and README data.
Needs a real Xget base URL before applying live configuration.
Only converts URLs that match the current Xget platform map.
Persistent shell or project changes should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Configure package manager acceleration
use_cases.description: Set up npm, pip, Cargo, NuGet, Conda, or similar tools to use an Xget deployment.

use_cases.title: Prepare container and CI workflows
use_cases.description: Adapt Docker, Kubernetes, and GitHub Actions examples to use Xget registry prefixes.

use_cases.title: Switch SDK base URLs
use_cases.description: Update AI SDK or inference API base URLs while keeping normal provider authentication behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Convert one URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this upstream URL to an Xget URL using my base URL: [base URL] and [upstream URL].

prompt_templates.title: List matching setup topics
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the current Xget use-case topics related to [tool or platform] and summarize the relevant setup path.

prompt_templates.title: Configure a project file
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this project to use Xget for [package manager or container registry]. Use [base URL] and verify the changed configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a multi-layer migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this repository and plan the Xget changes for package installs, container pulls, CI jobs, and SDK base URLs before applying them.

output_examples.input: Convert a GitHub repository URL using my Xget base URL.
output_examples.output: A rewritten Xget URL, the matched platform prefix, and a short note if the upstream URL is unsupported.

output_examples.input: Show Docker Compose guidance for my Xget deployment.
output_examples.output: A focused setup section with the public demo host replaced by the requested Xget host.

output_examples.input: Configure my project to use Xget for npm installs.
output_examples.output: A concise change summary, the affected configuration file, and a verification command or check result.

best_practices: Provide the real Xget base URL before asking for applied configuration changes.
Refresh the live platform map when exact prefixes or latest support matter.
Review persistent shell profile and project configuration changes before running them.
anti_patterns: Do not use the placeholder Xget host for production commands.
Do not assume an upstream URL is supported without checking the platform map.
Do not apply persistent profile changes when the user only asked for examples.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps adapt URLs, commands, and configuration so tools can use an Xget deployment.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper fetches live platform and README data when current Xget guidance is needed.

faq.question: Can it modify my project files?
faq.answer: Yes, when you ask it to apply configuration. Review changes before using persistent settings.

faq.question: Does it handle container registries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for OCI registry prefixes and container workflow configuration.

faq.question: Does it change API keys?
faq.answer: No. AI SDK guidance keeps normal provider authentication and changes only the relevant base URL.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is medium risk. The behavior is legitimate, but users should approve command execution and file changes.
